meaning: 'verily, really, indeed, moreover, much more, furthermore, however, only, just, merely, but rather, on the contrary, nevertheless'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. dāˀam 'but', Tna. dāˀam, dāˀā, dāˀa. Rundgren 1955:208, 217 analyzes it as *ˀəndā-ˀi-mu '< *ˀa-ˀa-ˀi-mu 'indem es nicht so ist > es ist vielmehr so'. The root passed into Cushitic: Bil. dāˀam 'but'.
ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. dāˀro 'sycamore'; from Cushitic: Sa. in-dāˀarō, Be. mindāˀro, Kham. dirunā.

meaning: (T), name of a sign of the zodiac; misprint for dabb.
origin_explanation: from Ar. dubb; also Amh. tallaq dabb 'southern star', tannəš dabb 'northern star'; from Ar. ˀad-dubb al-ˀakbar wad-dubb al-ˀaṣġar 'major and minor Ursa'. For dabb 'bear', see below.
SEMITIC: Ar. dubb 'bear', Heb. dob, Aram. dubbā, Syr. debbā, Akk. dubbu, dabū (West-Semitic loan-word). ETHIOPIC: Te.Amh. dabb, Tna. dabbī.

SEMITIC: onomatopoetic, as in Ar. dabḍāb 'drum', Soq. debābeh; related to Ar. duff 'tambourine', Heb. top, Aram. tuppā. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. dābay 'big drum', Amh. dabbe 'flat drum used by sorcerers or minstrels', dabb ˀanbāssa 'big drum' (from dabb 'drum' and ˀanbāssa 'lion', as is also the case of G. dabb ˀanbasā), Gur. dibbe 'kind of drum'; also in Cushitic: Or. dibbē 'drum'. See also dabḍaba I.

meaning: 'upon, on, onto, above, over, against, in opposition to, concerning, on account of'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. dib 'on, over, to'; connected with Har. dabḅa 'raised ground'; from Cushitic: Qem. dābā 'hill', Qua. debā, Bil. dibbā (so also Brockelmann 1950:38). Rundgren 1955:221 analyzes diba as coming from di-ba. See also dayyaba, below.

meaning: (K) 'den, cage, rope to tie animals, (K) snare, trap'

origin_explanation: from dabakʷˀəl (above) augmented by n.
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh.Gur. dābūb 'south'; passed into Cushitic: Kham. dibub 'south'. For the same noun expressing different directions, see ˀazeb, samen.

meaning: 'large umbrella held over the king or the ark, baldachin, dais, canopy, small roof over a gate'; dababa, subj. yədəbab (K*T.M), dabbaba (KG) 'open an umbrella, spread, distend' (denominative);

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh. dabab 'umbrella that priests carry in procession', Gur. (Zway) dabab, Amh. dababä 'shade the earth (cloud), give shade (tree)'; passed into Cushitic: Or. dibabä 'umbrella', but probably common with Bil. dababä (pl. dabab) 'roof'. For G. tadbab, cp. also Amh. tädbab.

SEMITIC: Ar. dababada 'resound (trampling), trample'. ETHIOPIC: Te. dabab bela 'trample', Amh. dabädabä 'beat, strike, thresh grain, resound', Gur. dabädabä 'hit, strike', dabbe 'stick used for hitting a ball'; also in Cushitic: Af. dabb 'sound produced by clapping hands', Som. dubba 'hammer'. See also dabb 'anbasä.

phonetics: 'be restless, (T.Y.M) be sad, worry, be troubled, be inactive, be feeble, be dazed, look haggard', (T) = Amh. adäkkämä 'weaken' (should read = Amh. däkkämä 'be weak');
origin_explanation: dababdbä, causative; dababdabä (Lt) 'apprehension, anxiety, uneasiness of mind'; SEMITIC: Ar. dababdaba 'crawl, hesitate in speaking' (for which see Dozy). ETHIOPIC: Gur. (tä)dabädabä 'hesitate, falter, be confused'; also in Cushitic: Qab. dabädbä-yo 'stammer'.

SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat) dabb 'fill up'. ETHIOPIC: Te. dabädbä 'cram a measure', Amh. dabädabä 'begin to swell or fill out (ear of grain)', (tän)dabädabä 'swell, form blisters', Gur. dabädabä 'be distended (stomach)'; also in Cushitic: Had. dabä-kko 'be swollen'.
from Amh. dabädabä 'patch'; also in Gur. dabädabä. Har. dabäb 'patch' (n); in Cushitic: Qab. dibu-ta.
from Amh. dabädabde 'letter'; also Tna. dabädabde passed into Cushitic: Bil. dabädabä 'letter, document'.

phonetics: (T) dibdiyaqon 'two-leaved tablets on the names of dead bishops and saints are written, diptych';
origin_explanation: from Gk. diptykhon 'diptych'; also in Chr.-Ar. dibdiqä 'prayer of intercession'.
from Ar. dibag 'brocade' < Pers. debag, depag (Eilers 605); also in Syr. dibaga, Md. dibas.

tadabala, (M) tadabala 'come together, assemble, hold a meeting'; dabəl (M) 'bunch, bundle'; madbal 'assembly, assemblage, gathering, lection, crowd, chorus'; SEMITIC: Ar. dabala 'unite', Soq. dbL. For the various meanings of this root in the Semitic languages see Nyberg 219. ETHIOPIC: Te. däbäbla 'stick together', Amh. däbäbla 'add, put together', Gur. däbäbla; with metathesis in Tna. dälläbä 'accumulate'. Te. däbäla passed into Cushitic: Be. debil 'gather, collect', Or. dabala 'repeat, add'. The connection with Bil. däbäl 'ein Tuch zusammenlegen um es als Leihgürtel umzubinden' suggested by Reinisch 1887:95 is unlikely. See also madbala.

phonetics: 'billy goat, bull, male of any animal';
origin_explanation: däbäla za-täli 'billy goat'; SEMITIC: Dillmann 1101 (followed by Nyberg 219) connects it with Ar. dawbal 'wild boar, suckling pig, fox, wolf'.

phonetics: 'ram', Tna. dibäla, Amh. dabäla, däbäl 'billy goat';
origin_explanation: from Cushitic: Sa.Af. dabälä (so also Brockelmann 1950:22); cp. also Har. däbälä 'heifer', Gur. zäbay (with alternance d:z), the form la of the various Ethiopian languages would then be palatalized in Harari and Gurage; also in Cushitic: Som. dibi 'calf', Or. dibicca 'young bull'.
transcription of Heb. däbelim (pl. of däbelä), same.
probably from Amh. däbäna, same; dabanänsä.

from Amh. däbanänsa, from däbän 'hair, grime, dirt' and ansa 'pick up', the expression means 'pick up the dirt!' (so DTW 333). Streleyn 1955:342 (quoting other sources) suggests dabbäl 'enlevé le fardeau!' (Amharic), or däbän ansa 'sorciers qui érigeaient la tente royale' (from däbäna and Amh. nässa).
ETHIOPIC: Tna. däbbäqä 'hide, conceal', Amh. bäqä 'hide', tädäbbäqä 'lie in ambush', Har. däbäqa 'hide, conceal'.

SEMITIC: Ar. dubr 'back', dabara 'turn the back', Heb. dibber 'turn the back' (Eitan 33-4), 'recline' (Dahood 1968:227), Md. dibra 'back, tail'; to be connected with Ar. dabara 'follow' (that is, 'be back of someone'). The connection with G. dabr 'mountain' originally *Bergrücken* suggested by B. mann 1102 (followed by Hommel 1877:XXVII) unlikely. Likewise doubtful is the identification with Akk. duppuru 'absent oneself, withdraw' with Ar. dubr 'back', as suggested by von Soden 177. ETHIOPIC: Tna. (tä)däbärä 'be inclined', Te. (tä)bära 'be placed on the side (in order to be killed)'. See also dabarbər.
dabr (pl. däbräät, däbrät) 'mountain, region where there is a monastery, convent, monastery';

phonetics: (Lt) 'a holy mountain in which the fallen angels lived before the deluge';
meaning: (dabra hagar, dabra 'ahgur 'metropolis'); (zadabra täbor 'Feast of the Transfiguration, celebrated on the 13th of Nahase'); (dabra zayt 'Mount of Olives'); (zadabra zayt 'the fourth Sunday in Lent'); 'adbräät 'a bishop's jurisdiction'; dabräwi (Gr.319) 'mountainous';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. dabra 'cultivated field' (from Ar. dabr 'mountain' coming from Geez; see Lane 845; Littmann 1964:353 connects G. dabr 'mountain' with Ar. dabr, dubr 'back, hinder part'), Heb. dober 'pasture', Aram. dabrä 'pasture, field', Syr. dabrä 'field', Md. dibra 'wilderness, desert, field', Ug. dbr 'steppe'. For the meanings 'mountain, field', cp. Akk. sadu 'mountain', Heb. sade 'field'. For the root dbr in Semitic, see T. Plassmann, CBQ 4 (1942).119-32; for Ethiopic, see p. 132. ETHIOPIC: Te. däbər 'mountain', Tna. däbri 'mountain, monastery', Gaf. däbrä 'mountain, forest', Gur. däbər, Amh. däbər 'monastery'. The connection between 'mountain' and 'monastery' can be explained by the fact that monasteries are usually on mountains. The root passed into Cushitic: Sa. dabər 'mountain'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. dänbär, dämbär 'territory, frontier', Te. dämbär 'boundary', Amh. dänbär, dämbär, dämbär, Gur. dämbär, a root augmented by n.
from Gk. dabir, transcription of Heb. däbir 'back room of a temple'.

phonetics: 'heap of earth on which monks lean for sleeping, backrest, inclining place for the back, lying on the back';
origin_explanation: a 1.2.3.2.3 root developed from dbr, above.
from Ar. 'ad-dabaran 'the Hyades, five stars of Taurus' (for which see Kunitzsch 1959, 109, 1961:51).

meaning: (K*) 'be well-developed, be well-grown (child), engage in illicit sex'; dabara (K*) 'engaging (n.) in illicit sex'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. dabbārā 'be well-developed, be well-grown (child)'. Could there be any connection with 'andabārā, above'?
transcription of Heb. dibre hayyāmim, same; also in Syr. dabār yāmīn. See also daryāmīn, baryāmīn.

SEMITIC: Ar. dibs 'honey', Sar. dbs., Šh. debš, Heb. dəbāš, Aram. dəbāšā, Syr. debšā, Md. dubša, Akk. dišpu. ETHIOPIC: Gaf. dābsā 'honey', Arg. dims, Har. dūs; Tna.Gur. dābšā 'dilute honey with water' (denominative).
from Ar. dabbūs, considered to be of Persian origin (see Landberg 1909:341, 1920:694; W. Marçais 1911:290). See also dǝmbus, dǝnbus.

meaning: (T.MA.M) 'be bent, walk bent over'; dǝbut (T) 'bent, bent over'.
(T.M) 'level, make level, (M) connect two banks of a river with a bridge'; dǝbut (T) 'leveled, evened';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. dābbāṭā 'press down (in measuring grain)'.
see dabawa I.
from Gk. diphthera 'skin, leather, parchment'; also in Amh. Tna. dābtär 'register', Te. däftär, Ar. daftar, Aram. diptārā.

meaning: (pl. dabtarāt, dabātār) 'tabernacle, (royal) tent, hut, pavilion, sanctuary, (by extension) unordained member of the church clergy who is well-versed in traditional church learning and who performs the hymns and sacred dances during the Mass, cantor, chorister'; (dabtarā marțul 'tabernacle, Holy of Holies'); (dabtarā sǝm 'tabernacle'); (kāhnāta dabtarā 'clergy of the royal or liqa dabtarā 'provost of the tabernacle'); adabtarā, (KG) dabtarā 'spread as an or as a tent' (denominative); tadabtarā 'be spread as an awning, be pitched (tent)'; dabtūr (K) 'spread as an awning, (tent)'; dabtāre (K*) 'spreading as a tent, (Lt) dabtarāwi 'one who makes tents';
origin_explanation: from Gk. diphthera 'skin (used as a tent), (see also dabtar, above); also in Te.Tna.Ar. tāra 'unordained member of the clergy', Amh. dābtarā 'tent' (from Geez). For Ar. daftar 'album' Fischer 1953:39 suggests either a Persian or a Greek origin. On the dabtara, see Walker 115-6; Lif. Chitz 9; Ullendorff 1968:91-2; D. Levine 171-3; E. Hammerschmidt, in Assfalg-Krüger 99-100; Velat, Les Cahiers coptes (Le Caire), no. 5, 1954.
dǝbut a-ba, ha-dǝbut (D.K*) 'secretly, quietly, expectantly, opportunely, properly, decently'; see also dabaya.
ETHIOPIC: related to Amh. dābäyyā 'stamp the earth'. Note also the Amharic expression ǝnqälf dābbāṭā-w 'sleep pressed down on him'.

meaning: (yǝdbi) (T.Lt) 'hide, lie in wait, approach stealthily, attack by surprise, fall upon suddenly, assault, pillage, invade, destroy, throw down, (K*) play a trick on someone'; tadabaya (Lt) 'be struck, be attacked'; tadābäya 'attack, assault'; dabāyi, act. part. of dabaya; also 'invader';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat) dabā (dby) 'creep', dabb (dby) 'surprise', Aram. dabā (dby) 'lie in wait'. ETHIOPIC: Te. dāba (dby) 'waylay, be hidden', dābä 'hide in the ground', Tna. ('a)dābä 'hide in wait', dābädābä 'hide in the ground', Amh. 'hide, lie in wait', dābba (db?) 'play wicked tricks on someone', dābäyyā 'ambush'; related to Gur. ('a)dāfäfā 'crouch down to lie in wait'. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Bil. dib-s 'hide', Qua. dǝb 'bury'. The derivation of the Ethiopian root from Or. ripa. əripa 'hide' suggested by Praetorius, ZDMG 43 (1889). 322-3 is unlikely. See also diba dabawa I, depa.

meaning: (pl. dedāt, dedeyāt, dedayāt, dedāyāt) 'door, courtyard, gate, vestibule, porch, open space (in front of a church)'; (dede barhān 'outbuilding on the eastern side of a church where the Host is prepared'); (dede salām 'small building to the left of the western entrance to a church where food is distributed');
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the identification with Heb. dalte (plural construct state) 'doors' > dede suggested by Bezold in Dillmann 1907:286, n.2 (so also Brockelmann 1908:179) is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Amh. dāgg 'door', Te. dāgā (< dāgg Praetorius 1871:50, possibly by pseudo-correction of d > ğ > g). The identification of dede with ədde 'door' suggested by Littmann-Höfner 383 is doubtful.
see wdd.
SEMITIC: Ar. dawdā 'cries, shouts, noises'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. (tän)d'add'a 'be heard (series of sounds)'. See also dodǝwo.

meaning: (T.Sw. in D 1398) 'fire for burning cleared-off trees and bushes of a field'.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: possibly a 'Lallwort' (see also Noeldekke 1910:121), as in Ar. dāda 'wet nurse', Sar. (Qataban) dd 'uncle', Soq. dedo, Heb. dōd 'beloved, uncle', Aram. dōdā, Syr. dādā, Md. dada, Ug. 'love', Akk. dādu 'favorite, darling'. Some of these nouns may be derived from the root wdd 'love'. ETHIOPIC: Gur. dade, form of address to an old man or to an elder relative; also Cushitic: Had. Kam. dadda.

from dak '(wahrscheinlich) was den Boden stampft und breit, hart macht' suggested by Růžička 196 is unlikely. Reinisch 1890: 101 compares Sa. dadā 'rainy season', but the phonetic correspondence is not normal.
see also dǝdarkǝm, below.

meaning: (KBT 1026) 'desert, dry land', (T) = Amh. yāmādǝr bota 'place of the land';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. dudaleb, dudalem 'center of the world, Jerusalem, dry land, desert', from dud (= dǝd) 'foundation' and alām 'world'. Tayyā's translation 'place of the land' is strange.

meaning: (K*) = Amh. mīnta 'twin, fork of a road';
origin_explanation: from Gk. didymos 'twin, twofold'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. dīdān, dīdän 'fierce and cold wind'.

meaning: 'happen, occur suddenly, come upon unexpectedly, fall upon, crush, press upon, knock down, strike hard, (T) attack by surprise'; dādāqa 'knock down, strike'; tadaddaqqa, see tadādāqa; tadādāqa 'happen, come upon, happen to arrive, happen by chance, befall, fall upon, appear, occur, surprise, meet, rush into'; dādǝq 'accident, chance, event (unforeseen), calamity, that which befalls, (T.M) contagious disease, fever'; (dādǝqa 'perchance'); dadaq, see dǝdǝq; daddāqi (Lt) 'sudden';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: from dǝdq, from wdq 'fall > fall upon, happen' (Dillmann 1907:132); Amh. dādāqā 'pursue and suddenly attack an enemy'; perhaps also Gur. dudǝq balā- 'be confused, not know what to do, be surprised, wonder'. Růžička 197 connects *dādǝq with Ar. daqqa 'break, bruise' (see dqq, below). H. Bauer, ZS 10 (1935). 176 derives daddaqqa from *tawdāqa > *dadāqa 'fall' (that is, a 'verkäpptes Reflexivum') > *dawdaqa > daddaqqa.
from dandar > ded(d)er.
is there any connection with dīdrakǝm 'double drachma' conceived as weight? See also dǝdkǝme.
from Gk. didrachmon 'two-drachma coin'; also Syr. didarakmā. See also dǝdarkam, darkame, dǝrham.
perhaps from Gk. *didarkanos* 'double purple' (W. Müller). See also darakano.
see dǝd II.

SEMITIC: Ar. *da'da'a* 'run fast'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. *daddǝde* 'steps taken by a baby learn-ing to walk'; Te. *daddǝ'a* 'run quickly', probably also *deda* 'take by the hand' (if it refers to training a child to walk).
SEMITIC: related to Ar. *daff* 'leaf of a door'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. *däf*, *däfi* 'threshold', Gur. *däf* 'doorframe'; passed into Cushitic: Kam. *defa*, Or. *dafu*.

SEMITIC: Ar. *daf'a* 'a push' (on the various Arabic dialects, see W. Marçais 1959:1289ff), Sh. *defa'*; related to Akk. *da'apu* 'push'. ETHIOPIC: Te. *däf'a* 'push', Tna. *däf'e* 'push', *däf'e* 'crush, squash', Amh. *däf'a* 'push', Gur. *däf'a* 'turn on its side (e.g. a vessel when emptying ma-nure)'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. *däfdäfä* 'put earth on top of thatch', Amh. *däfaddäfä* 'coat, plaster, besmear (with oint-ment)'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. *däfaddäfä* 'mix water with ingredi-ents such as gešo-plant, ground barley sprouts to make the must for wine', *dafdaf* 'must of wine', Gur. *däfaddäfä* 'brew beer'.

meaning: 'cover, hide, bury, obstruct, lay aside, block, bake (under ashes)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. *dafana* 'hide, cover, bury', S.Ar. *mdfn* 'corn-storage pit', Ar. (Yem) *madfan*, So. *defon* 'bury'. For Syriac, see *dofoni*, below. ETHIOPIC: Te. *däfna* 'cover, block up', Tna. *däfnä*, Amh. *däffänä*, Arg. *däffäna*, Har. *däfäna*, *däfänä*. For *däfənt* 'cake baked under ashes', *dafana* 'cover', cp. Amh. *däfuni* 'a wheaten (cooked under ashes)', Gur. *däfun* 'bread of inferior quality' (lit. 'covered'), Syr. *ṭmīrtā* 'cake', *ṭmār* 'hide'. The root passed into Cushitic. Bil. *däfan* 'bury', Aw. *defänu-k*a. See also *dafnaṭa*.
from Gk. *daphnē* 'laurel'; also in Syr. *dapnē*, Aram. *dapnā*.
from Syr. *dupna* which perhaps goes back to *dafana*, above.
denominative from *däfənt* (see *dafana*).

meaning: (T*) = Amh. *qammo* 'a tree whose fruit grows in bunches and is edible or raw'.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. *däfär* 'hill, height that blocks the view'.

meaning: 'dare, be bold, be audacious, be insolent, be irreverent, affront (dafara lävəla 'treat with insolence')';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. *dafara* 'push', Akk. *dapāru* 'become sated' (for the passage of meaning 'be sated > be bold, be insolent', cp. Amh. *ṭäggäbä* 'be sated, be arrogant, be insolent'). ETHIOPIC: Tna. *däfärä* 'be daring, be bold, be in-solent', Te. *däfra*, Amh. *däfärä*, Arg. *däffära*, Gur. *däfärä*; passed into Cushitic: Bil.Qua. *däfär* 'be insolent', Aw. *defär-g*a.

meaning: 'kind of tree' (T) = Amh. *qärät* 'kind of tree (Osyris compressa)';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Syr. *daprānā* 'juniper', Ug. *dprn*, Akk. *daprānu*.
ETHIOPIC: perhaps to be compared with Amh.Gur. *däffit* 'snare'.

SEMITIC: related to Aram. *ṭiktā* 'drawstring, waist-band', Syr. *tektā*, Md. *ṭakta*. Arabic *dikka*, *tikkä* is considered an Aramaic loanword (Fraenkel 55). ETHIOPIC: Amh. *dag(g)* 'a sort of sash of cloth wrapped around the body over the clothing from chest to waist'. M. Cohen 1931:109 compares *dag(g)* with Gur. *däqot* 'belt' (cp. also Gaf. *däq* 'ätä), *d-q* (Gur.Gaf.) > *d-g* (Amh.G). also in Cushitic: Aw. *dungi* 'belt'. See also *dagdaga* III.

meaning: 'antiphonary supplied with cantilla-tion signs indicating the proper intonation of hymns';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. *dagg*a; see Strelcyn 1974:6, Ham-merschmidt 1977:90. See also (*swm*), *som*.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. *däg*a 'a highland', Amh. *däga*, Gaf. *däga*, *dägi*, Har. *däg* 'elevated ground'; from Cushitic: Ic.Sa.Af. *daga* 'highland', Bil. *dag* 'on, above', Qua. *däg*.

meaning: 'kind of palm, palm leaves, (T) garment of palm leaves'.

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. *däg*ä'abä (also *godäbo*, with metathe-sis) 'kind of cricket', Te. *dängäbab* (instead of *däng*a-bät in Reinisch 1887:108). Amh. *dägobe*, *dägobäya* 'wingless locust'; from Cushitic: Bil. *däng* 'abe, *g*än-däbe* 'locust' (Brockelmann 1950:28). However, it may be possible that the Te. root *dängäba* 'cut through, cut off' is the origin of the noun for 'cricket, locust'.

meaning: 'be lean, be meager, be ema-ciated, (Lt) waste away, wither away';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat) *dagg* 'grind to a powder'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. *dägaddägä* 'pulverize, become thin, be emaciated'. For 'beat until small', cp. Te. *dägdä-ga* 'crush under foot' > 'make meager, emaciated', Tna. *dägdägä* 'trample, step on', Gur. *dägädägä* 'squeeze'.

meaning: (T.Y.MA.M) 'be early morn-ing, get up early in the morning';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. *dägaddägä* 'get up early in the morning, hasten off' (from Geez). The verb is per-haps a denominative from *dägdag* 'cock' (see below) whose crowing indicates the early morning.
*adagdaga* (K*), causative;

ETHIOPIC. Tna. `adagdaga` ‘gird oneself’, Amh. `(a)daggadaga` ‘wear the samma-garment around the waist’. The verb is either a reduplicative of `dagga` ‘belt’ (above) or a development of meaning of `dagdaga` II ‘get up early in the morning > gird oneself and be ready to move’. For a similar development, cp. Heb. `hiskim` ‘rise early, do something early’, denominative from `sekem` ‘shoulder’, the verb mean- ing ‘load (in the early morning) the back of a beast > get up early, do something early in the morning’.
SEMITIC: Ar. `daqdaq` ‘domestic fowls: cocks, hens, chickens’, from an onomatopoetic `dgdg`, for which cp. Sh. `edegdeg` ‘make a tapping noise’. See also `dagdaga` II.
ETHIOPIC. Amh. `(as)dagdaddaga` ‘frighten, cause to be afraid’.
ETHIOPIC. Tna.Amh. `daggäfa` ‘sustain, prop, sup- port one’s family’. Te. `dägfa`, Arg. `deggäfa`, Gur. `deggäfa`, passed into Cushitic: Bil. `dägäfa` ‘sustain’, Aw. `degäf-k`a’.
ETHIOPIC. Amh. `daggägä` ‘be proud, be arrogant’.
ETHIOPIC. Te. `dagha` ‘strike’ is not to be connected with G. `zäg ha` ‘close’, as suggested by Littmann- Höfner 541. The root passed into Cushitic: Bil. `dagah` ‘strike’ (from Tigre).
probably wrongly taken from `danqal` = Amh. `gaga- no` ‘ibis’, 4 lines above `dagal` in Tayyä 101.
metathesis in relation to `dalg` `amma` II, below.

meaning: ‘repeat, do a second time, be again, recite prayers or incantations’;
origin_explanation: `dagama` + verb expresses ‘again, a second time’ of the action of the verb); `adgama` (K*), causative; `tadgama`, pass. of `dagama`; `dagami`, act. part. of `dagama`; `dagum` (K*) ‘repeated, recited’; `dägäm` (fem. `dägämt`) ‘second, other, fur- ther’ (`dägäm` `orit`, `orit zadägäm` ‘Deuteronomy’); (`dägäm` `ladat` ‘regeneration’); (`dägäma`, `ba-dägäm` ‘twice, for the second time, secondly, again, further, furthermore, more, moreover’); (`vidägäma` ‘no more, never more, no longer’); `dägäm` (K*) ‘repeating, reciting the prayers’ (n); `dagimot` ‘repetition’; `dägämäwi`, `dägämäy` (fem. `dägämäwit`, `dägämit`) ‘second’; `mədägäm` ‘place, time, or book for reciting, a piece in the `dagg`a` to be repeated’ (Velat 469). ETHIOPIC: Te. `dägma` ‘repeat’, `dägäm` ‘second infu- sion of coffee’. Tna. `dägämä` ‘repeat, recite’, `dägämä` ‘repeat, add, recite prayers’, `dägäm` ‘sec- ond’, `dägmo`, `dämmo` ‘again’. Har. `dägän` `asä` ‘review a lesson’ (that is, ‘do it a second time’). The root passed into Cushitic: Bil. `dägäm` ‘repeat’.

SEMITIC: Ar. `dagr` ‘wood of the plough to which the plowshare is attached’ ETHIOPIC: Tna. `dugri`, `dag`ri` ‘plough beam’, Amh. Gur. `dägär`; also in Cushitic: Be. `dugure`, Sa. `dugre`, Or. `digre`.

meaning: ‘prick, puncture, goad, poke, prod, transfix, pierce, sting, smite, punch, wound’;
origin_explanation: `tadag`sa`, passive; `astadag`sa` (K*), reciprocal of `dag`asa`; `vastadag`sa` ‘slander, express enmity through slander, cause dispute, sow discord’; `dag`asi`, act. part. of `dag`asa`; `dag`us`, pass. part. of `dag`asa`; `dag`sat`, verbal noun of `dag`asa`; `madg`as`, `madgus`, `madg`as` ‘goad’; `mastadag`as`, part. of `vastadag`sa` SEMITIC: the closest root is Aram.-Syr. `davas` ‘prick, thrust in’ (so also Dillmann 1133), but phonetic correspondence is not regular. ETHIOPIC: Amh. `mädg`as` ‘cattle prod’ (from Geez).

meaning: ‘arrange, arrange well, prepare (food for a wedding feast), deco- rate, cover with leather’;
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC. Amh. `dagg`asa` ‘cover with leather, make designs on a book cover’ (note Amh. `dg`s, with `s` as against G. `dg`s, with `s`).

meaning: = Amh. `g`ayya` ‘kind of pea believed to cause paralysis of the legs if eaten with milk or linseed’.

origin_explanation: misprint for `dahab`, from Ar. `dahab`; also Te.Tna. `dahab` ‘gold’.

meaning: for `madä- hamt`, for which see `damha` II.
‘demolish, destroy (K) dig, scratch the ground, cultivate, pile up (grain), make a bed (for flowers, vegetables)’;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Praetorius, ZDMG 57 (1903). 275 connects it with Ar. `hadama` ‘demolish, pull down’ (with metathesis and reduplication of the last radical). He also identifies it with Ar. `(Dat) hadmala` and `dah- mala` ‘destroy’ (with metathesis `hdm:dhm`, and augmentation with `l`).
from Ar. `dahira` ‘ammunition for war’; also Amh. `dahera`.

meaning: ‘perish, be wiped out, be spoiled, (K.DTW 374) push aside, re- pulse’;
origin_explanation: `dähf` ‘pushing out, expulsion, plundering, (T.M) ruin, disaster, perdition, corruption’; SEMITIC: Heb. `dähap` ‘push away, knock off’, Aram. `dap`, Akk. `da`apu` (von Soden 146); related to Ar. `dahaf` ‘push’ (see Landberg 1920:860). ETHIOPIC: Tna. `dahafa` ‘damage, harm, injure’, Amh. `dafa` ‘push, push over, shove’, related to Amh. `däffa` (`df?`) ‘push over’, Gur. `däf` `balä` ‘disap- pear, be extinguished’.

meaning: ‘creep, crawl, go on all fours, be lame, limp, hesitate (in speaking)’;
origin_explanation: `adhaka`, causative; `dähuk` (K*) ‘one who crawls, who creeps’; `mədähak` (K*) ‘place where one creeps’ SEMITIC: related to Ar. `dahaqa` ‘drag on the ground’ (Dillmann 1084). ETHIOPIC: Te. `dähaka` ‘crawl on hands and knees’, Tna. `dähaka` ‘stamp’, Amh.Gur. `daha`, `daka`.

`tadähla`, (L.Gr.313) `tadähla`, (T) `tadähla` ‘with- draw, retreat, retire, flee, be put to flight, run away, (K) be defeated’; `dähl` (K*) ‘fleeing’ (n); SEMITIC: Ar. `dahala` ‘remove to a distance, flee’. The connection with Ug. `dhl` ‘fear’ (Fisher 65), and Syr. `dhel` ‘fear’ (Appleyard 54) is unlikely. Schulthess 66-7 likewise connects various Semitic roots mean- ing ‘fear’ and ‘flee’. See on this root H. Rabin, IOS 2 (1972).363, 367. ETHIOPIC: Te. `däha` ‘reject, drive away’ (with me- tathesis), Amh. `dälla` (from `dl?`) ‘flee’, cp. also Tna. Amh.Gur. `däl` ‘victory’, Har. `dili`. The noun `däl`, `dili` would then mean ‘act of making someone flee’, that

meaning: (K) ‘curved, winding, bent, turned the wrong way, bow (n)’;
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC. Amh.Gur. `dägän` ‘carding bow’, Har. `dägän` ‘bow’, (`tä)dägäna` ‘be bent’; also in Cushitic: Som. `degan` ‘carding bow’, Aw. `dägän`, Sid.Dar. `digäna`.
see also `tegan`, `tegan` I.

`adegana`, causative; `degani`, (KG) `degän`, act. part. of `degana` SEMITIC: the connection with Syr. `degän` ‘be blurry (eyes)’ suggested by Brockelmann 1928:142 is un- likely. ETHIOPIC: Tna. `dägäna` ‘pursue’, Te. `dägna` ‘chase, way’, (`tä)dägäna` ‘persecute’, Amh. `dägäna` ‘track’ (hunter; from Geez). Reinisch 1902:105 com- pares Som. (`mi)dägän` ‘hunter’; cp. also Bil. `dägän` ‘chase away’, Sid. `dugannä` ‘hunter’.

meaning: 'a little stick used for striking a stringed instrument'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. daḥanṣā (from Geez).

meaning: 'divorce, repudiate (a wife), send away (a wife)'; tadāḥra (K*) 'be divorced'; daḥart (fem. of *daḥur) 'divorced, repudiated'; dāḥarot 'divorce'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. daḥara 'drive away, banish, repudiate'. ETHIOPIC: Te. (tä)dāḥara 'be separated, be kept apart'; passed into Cushitic: Qua. daḥar 'divorce'; perhaps Bil. daqar.

meaning: (Lt) 'be trampled down, be trod on'; 'adḥasot (Lt) 'swift movement of the feet'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. daḥasa 'search by stirring the ground with the feet', Ar. (Oman) daḥas 'destroy'; related to Ar. daḥasa 'trample, crush', da'rasa 'trample down, crush'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. dāḥasā 'destroy, demolish', Te. dāḥasā 'tread down', Amh. dasā 'step on, trample, demolish', Gur. dasā 'demolish, destroy'. See also dasaya.

meaning: 'glide, slide, slip, trip, stumble, totter, miscarry'; 'adḥadā 'let slip, let slide, miscarry, make miscarry'; daḥdā, act. part. of daḥda; daḥud 'one who slides, one who slips'; dāḥd, daḥad, (L) daḥād 'sliding (n), slipping, downfall, slippery ground, slippery thing, stumbling block, error, obstacle, impediment, abortion'; daḥdāt 'slipping (n), tottering, blunder'; madḥad, madḥad, (K*) madḥadi 'slipping (n), sliding, (K) that which makes slip, that which makes slide, slippery'; madḥād 'slippery place'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. daḥada 'slip', 'adḥada 'make slippery', Ar. (Yemen) taḥaṣ 'slip', Soq. daḥad 'slide'.

meaning: 'slip, stumble', Tna. dāḥaṣā (with augmented I), Amh.Gur. glide, trample, crush'.
origin_explanation: The derivation of H. daḥaṣa from dhd suggested by Littmann, ZA 33 (1921), 108 is unlikely. The root dḥd, dḥd is undoubtedly connected with Bil. galḥat 'slide'.

meaning: 'excavate, hollow out'; daḥuḥ 'hollow, excavated, concave, chasm, cave';
origin_explanation: related to daḥaya.

phonetics: daḥaha II (yədḥaḥ) (Y.M.T in the reprint), daḥaha (MA)
meaning: 'be strong, be solid, be high'; dāḥuḥ (T) 'strong';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. daḥūḥ 'big, enormous (woman, cattle)'.

meaning: 'be saved, be unharmed, escape safely, be spared, be safe and sound, be released'; 'adḥana 'save, keep safe, deliver, rescue, be a guarantor'; tadḥana 'act as a guarantor (for a person)', (zatadāḥna 'the person for whom a guaranty is given'); daḥun 'safe and sound, wholesome, unharmed, immune (to)'; (daḥuna 'safely, peacefully'); daḥin 'deliverance, salvation'; dāḥn, daḥn 'safe, sound and well, wholesome, unharmed, (Gr.476) innocent, not guilty; safety, well-being, security, immunity'; (daḥna, ba-dāḥn 'peacefully, safely'); dāḥnā (dāḥnā) 'health, safety, safeguard, sound-integrity, welfare, security'; (ba-dāḥnā 'safely'); daḥnat 'salvation, deliverance, integrity'; (ṣoma daḥnat 'Fast of Wednesday and Friday'); 'adḥani, act. part. of 'adḥana; also 'savior'; 'adḥano (t) 'salvation, deliverance, guaranty (for a person)'; ('adḥano za-nafs 'guaranty for a person'); madḥan 'redeemer, savior, saving (adj), deliverer, guarantor for a person'; (madḥana nafs 'guarantor for a person'); madḥani 'redeeming (adj), redeemer, savior'; (madḥane 'ālam 'Christ', lit. 'savior of the world'); madḥanit 'salvation, deliverance, safety, redemption, medicine'; madḥanāwi 'salvation'; madḥanāwi 'saving (adj)'; tadḥano (t) 'guaranty for a person'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Praetorius 1890:45-6 wrongly suggests two roots: dḥn I 'be saved', and dḥn II 'guaranty'. His identification of dḥn 'guaranty' with Ar. raḥana 'pledge' is unlikely. Nor is his identification of dḥn 'be saved' with the Semitic root ḥn 'enjoy', with a prefixed t- becoming tḥn (with agglutinated t > dḥn, dḥn, convincing. ETHIOPIC: Tna. dāḥanā 'be safe, be well', Te. dāḥana, Amh. danā 'be saved, be spared (life)', dāḥna 'well' (from Amharic). For G. madḥan 'guaran tor', cp. Tna. mādḥan, Amh. mādḥan, Gur. mādḥan. The root passed into Cushitic: Qua. dāḥanā 'be saved, be in good health', Kham. diḥen, Be. deḥan, madan 'guarantor'.

meaning: 'be behind, follow behind, be late, be last'; daḥara 'delay'; 'adḥara, 'adāḥara 'delay, defer, retard, leave behind, (T) cause to follow'; tadāḥara 'remain behind, be behind, stay away, tarry, draw back, loiter, linger, be delayed, be late, come at a late hour'; 'astadāḥara, 'astadāḥara 'preserve to the last, reserve, keep back, restrain, postpone, defer, delay, retard, find oneself lagging behind, linger'; daḥārit (fem. daḥārit, pl. m. daḥārāyān, pl. f. daḥārāyāt, pl. com. daḥart) 'last, latter, posterior, rear, latest, hindmost'; (za-daḥāri 'last, farthest'); (ba-daḥāri 'later on'); dāḥra (Gr) 'then, afterwards'; dāḥr (Gr.476) dāḥr 'rear, posterior, back, past (time)'; ('adḥra 'behind, back, back of, backwards, afterwards, then, finally, at last, after, [adv], late'); (with suffix pronouns the base is dāḥre-; note dāḥrewu 'afterwards, thereafter'); (ba-dāḥra 'behind, after'); ('anta dāḥra 'behind'); ('amdāḥra 'from behind, after [time]'); ('amdāḥra [conj] 'after that'); ('amdāḥra-zā 'afterwards, later'); ('amdāḥra zəntuṣṣa 'from now on'); daḥārit 'end, posterity'; (daḥārita 'at the end, backwards, behind, in the back'); dāḥrit-a 'backward, backwards, in the back, behind'; dāḥərt 'end'; dāḥ(a)rāwi, dāḥ(a)rāy 'last, late, farthest'; daḥārāwi, daḥārāy, same; dāḥrənnā 'end'; tadāḥrot (n) 'being afterwards, being later on';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Soq. tḥar 'remain behind'. H. Bauer, ZS 10 (1935), 176 derives G. daḥra from Semitic t + ḥr (so also Dillmann 1108) with a 'verkapptes t-Reflexivum': *taḥara > *taḥara > dḥr (with t > d by assimilation due to r). Rundgren 1955:209 derives dḥra from *'anta 'oḥra > *'andəḥra > dōḥra. For 'ḥr, see 'aḥara, above. ETHIOPIC: Tna. dāḥarā 'delay, be late, remain behind', Te. tāḥara 'delay, retard', (tā)dāḥara 'be kept apart', Amh. daḥrā 'post-' as in dāḥrā maruq 'post-graduate' (dāḥrā coming from Geez). The connection with Te. dāḥara 'be low, be little' suggested by Littmann-Höfner 509 is unlikely. As for dōro 'previously' (Tna.Amh.Arg.Gur.), the suggestion made by Praetorius 1879:375 to derive it from dḥr, that is, 'something that is behind', is doubtful.

meaning: 'bless, invoke favor upon, sanction, allow, (K*) marry off'; tadāḥra, passive; daḥur 'blessed'; madḥar, madḥar 'one who blesses; benediction, blessing, sanction, (M) good omen';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the derivation of dhr (instead of dḥr) from *taḥwart (from a non-existing ḥwr in Geez 'be merciful') > *ḥr > dḥr suggested by Praetorius 1890:46 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Te. dāḥarā 'bless, implore blessing', Arg. dāḥarā, Gur. darā 'bless the newly married', Amh. darā 'marry off' (probably by giving a blessing).
Seems to represent Amh. das (see below) in a Geez form.

meaning: 'garment of a high priest, (T) silken turban'
origin_explanation: From Gk. dēlōsin (acc. sg. of dēlōsis) 'explanation, interpretation'. It renders Heb. 'ūrīm, and G. dilo-son seems to be wrongly translated by Tayyā as Amh. kufar 'white silk cloth, silken turban'.

meaning: 'weigh''adalawa 'cause to weigh'tadalawa, tadalawa 'be weighed';dalāwī 'one who weighs';dalāw (fem. dallut) 'weighed';dalwat 'weight, weighing';madlow (Lt) 'weight, balance';madlot (pl. madlāw) 'weight, measure, balance, scales, price, value, proportion';(madalawa dahāy 'sundial'; translation of Ar. mīzānu aš-šams);('abna madalāw 'balance');('ayna madalāw 'tongue of a balance');madlut 'price';madlāw (Lt), madlāwī (M) 'balance';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Sar. m-dlw, m-dlt 'weight'. Soq. dele 'weigh'. Dillmann 1078 (followed by others) con-nects it with Sem. dlw, dly 'draw water from a well': Heb. dālā, Ar. dalā, Ar. dalā, dālī, Syr. dalā, Akk. dalu. A. Ambros suggests for dlw/y an original meaning 'hinuntergehen lassen', that is, 'den Eimer in den Brunnen ebenso wie das gewogene Gut zur Gewichts-bestimmung an der Waage'. However, these verbs are denominatives from *dōlī 'pail' and there does not seem to be any connection between G. dlw 'weigh' and dlw, dly 'draw water from a well'. Praetorius 1890:32 note * considers it related to Ar. dalla (dll) 'indicate'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. dālāwā 'weigh', Amh. dāllā 'be measured out, be weighed', perhaps Te. dālā (dlw) 'know exactly' (that is, 'by weighing'). Praetorius 1879:132 connects it with the root dldl 'level' (Amh. Tna. Har. Gur). See also dalawa II.

meaning: 'be suitable, suit, be fitting, befit, be appropriate, be proper, be seemly, be correct, be becoming to, behoove, be convenient, be incumbent upon, be neces-sary, have to, be one's duty, deserve, be useful, be profitable, be agreeable'; with object suffix pronouns, 'deserve, be worthy of, be due to, be entitled to, should' (e.g. yadallāwom 'they deserve, they are worthy', yadallāwanni 'I should');(zayyadallu 'proper, seemly, appropriate');(zayyādallu 'unseemly, improper');(bazā'iyyādallu 'unduly');'adalawa, causatives; also 'please, satisfy, prepare, accept favorably, respect, revere, do favors, show partiality, take sides, flatter, late, be a hypocrite';(yadlawa lā-gaṣṣ, 'adlawa lā'ēla gaṣṣ 'show par-tiality to, show favor to, favor');tadallawa 'be prepared, get ready, be worthy, enjoy oneself, live in luxury, be a pleasure, be fortunate, have in abundance';'astadallawa, 'astadālāwa 'make ready, make preparations, prepare, cause to prepare, establish, make worthy, reserve, revere, (Lt) equal-ize';dallāw (fem. dallut) 'fit, fitting, proper, suitable, worthy, deserving, becoming, appropriate, due, successful, established, ready, prepared'; see also dallāwān, below;(dallawa, ba-dallāw 'worthily, becomingly, suitably, properly');dallāwān, pl. of dallāw (Gr.474.KBT) 'that which is proper, that which is right, special funeral rites' (so called because this word occurs in the section of prayers for this kind of funeral);dalwat 'being fit (n), fitness, being appropriate (n), propriety, worthiness, suitability, right, desert, merit';(dalwata, ba-dalwat 'duly, justly, rightly');tadlā 'pleasure, enjoyment, delight, rapture, luxury, charm, affluence, dignity, glory, suitability, propriety, appropriateness, prepara-tion';'adlāwo, 'adlāwo gaṣṣ 'doing favors (n), favoritism, flattery, partiality, hypocrisy, taking sides' (n);('i'adlāwo 'impartiality');'astadālāwī (Gr), part. of 'astadālāwa;madlāw, madlāw, madlāw, madlāwī 'hypocrite, unjust judge';madlut 'fitting manner, suitable manner';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. ('ad)dālāwā 'be partial', Amh. dāllā 'be comfortable, be convenient, be suitable', Amh. Gur. (a)dāllā 'show favor', Te. dālā (dlw) 'be ready', 'ab dālī 'indeed, certainly'. For Amh. aydollām 'he is not going back to dlw 'be convenient', see Cohen 1924:134, but see Leslau 1979:14. Praetorius, ZD-MG 62 (1908).753-4 is probably correct in combin-ing dalawa I 'weigh' with dalawa II 'be suit-able', that is to say, the concept 'weigh, have the correct weight' could have developed into that of something being suitable, correct, fitting, and so on. However, he wrongly separates the meaning 'get ready' (as tadallawa 'be ready', dallaw 'ready') from dalawa II and compares it with Ar. (Hadr. Dat) dallā 'begin'. See also mdlw.
From Ar. 'ad-dalw 'Water carrier'; also in Amh. dālāy nāfas, Aram.-Syr. dawlā, Md. daula. For Ar. 'ad-dalw, see Kunitzsch 1961:52.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. dālāyā 'wish, desire', Te. dālā (dly) 'care about'.

meaning: 'blood, sap'(dama nafs 'human or animal blood');('aba dam, ba'āla dam 'avenger of blood');(bə'əse dam 'bloodthirsty');(sutāfē dam 'relation by blood');damāwī (K*) 'pertaining to blood, bloody';dammāwa, see below
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. dam 'blood', SAr. dm, Heb. dam, Aram.-Syr. dmā, Md. dma, Ug. dm, Akk. dāmu. See on this biradical root Noeldeke 1910:117f. ETHIOPIC: dām.

meaning: 'reach, obtain, receive, attain, overtake, grasp, acquire, find'(M renders Y's adämmāqā [Amharic transla-tion of 'admə'a] 'make red-hot', but it could also mean 'cause to sing together with other singers' and possibly be a development of the meaning 'participate', as is the case in Te. ('ad)dāmə'a 'let participate', Tna. (tā)dām-mə'e 'participate in a culpable action of some-one'. See also damha I.

origin_explanation: probably from Amh. dāmburra, dāmburre 'cheap quality cloth made of cotton and other materials'.

meaning: 'club, iron mace, iron staff'; represents dambus, with inserted n, see dabbus. For mb < nb < bb, cp. also Ar. (Iraq) dambūs 'mace'.
origin_explanation: For mb < nb < bb, cp. also Ar. (Iraq) dambūs 'mace'.

meaning: 'let the hair grow long, have long hair''adamdama (Lt) 'adorn the head with knotted hair';damdāmā 'hair of head, long locks, (Sw. in D 1091) head, top of head';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. dāmaddāmā 'let the hair grow'; perhaps to be identified with dāmāh 'head' (see below) reduced to dm and reduplicated as dmdm.
SEMITIC: related to the meaning of Ar. damma 'make even'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. dāmaddāmā 'level off', Gur. damm barā, damdōmm barā 'be very close to one another'.

meaning: 'cause to be astonished or stupefied'; see dmm I, tadamma.

'resound with cries, shout, roar, make noise''admada 'make resound';damud 'resounding';damd 'voice, sound, loud noise, rumbling, ru-mor, report, noise, whisper';damdāwī (Gr) 'noisy, one with a good voice';'admādī (Gr.475), madmad (K*) 'that makes resound';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. 'admāsa 'sound, speak', Tna. 'ad-māsā, Amh. dāmmātā 'cry out, make noise', dams 'voice, sound'; passed into Cushitic: Qem. domīt 'sound'.

meaning: 'possess, acquire, domi-nate' related to dm', 'admə'a 'receive, acquire', above.
'dip, dunk, plunge, sink, draw with a ladle, scoop up with a basket, (Y.T.K) penetrate, know thoroughly, examine'

phonetics: (K), madməḥ (T), madməḥ, madməḥ (L.D), madməḥ (L), pl. madəməḥ, madəməḥ, madəməḥ (L)
meaning: 'ladle, cup, bowl, spoon for administering the Eucharist, grater'
'head, crown of the head, skull, summit'
origin_explanation: is either borrowed from Ar. dimāġ 'brain' or is connected with Ar. damāmaha 'incline the head', a denominative from *dimāḥ. The root also occurs in Amh. dəmāḥ 'top of the head' (probably from Geez), Arg. dəmāḥ 'head', Gaf. dəmāḥ, Gur. dum 'head, hair of the head', Te. dəmāqāt 'crown of the head' (with ğ > q in an Arabic loanword); in Cushitic: Aw. dūmi 'top', Or. dumi 'head'. W. Müller suggests that G. dəmāḥ (with ḥ) is the original form and considers it a cognate with Ar. dimāġ and not a loanword from Ar. dimāġ.

meaning: 'heifer (worshipped as idol), (T) idol in the form of a lion'; damala (MA) 'make an idol' (denominative)'
origin_explanation: from Gk. damalis 'young calf, heifer'; also in Amh. damali 'image or likeness of lion' (from Geez).

SEMITIC: originally 'be silent' > be deprived of speech > be stupefied, marvel', as in Heb. dmm 'be silent', PBh. dimdem 'to silence', Aram. dimdem 'lie in a stupor', Md. dndm (< dmdm) 'be deprived of speech or movement by emotion, stupefied', dmm 'come to a stop'; for Ug. dm 'be quiet'. J. Blau, VT 6 (1956).242-3 connects dmm I with dmm II. See on this root G. v. 'The stems dmm and dmn in Hebrew', JBL 32 (1913).219-43; N. Lohfink, VT 12 (1962).275-6. A bibliography dealing with the Hebrew root ib. 275, n.1.

meaning: 'close, cover, fill up, heap up, level'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. damma 'stop up (a hole), level'. ETHIOPIC: Te. damāma 'close the udder', Tna. dām 'finish', Gur. dəmāddämä 'block the mouth of someone, finish thatching a house'.

SEMITIC: Ar. damdama 'rumble', Heb. dəmām 'weep' (Dahood 1966:24-5), Ug. dmm 'grieve', Md. dmdm 'mutter, grumble', Akk. damāmu 'moan, mourn'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. dāmmä bäla 'resound, give forth a sound', Amh. damm alä, dämmāmā 'make (a)dämmāmā same. Gur. dämm bara 'make noise by falling', dəmədām balä, same.

meaning: 'cover with clouds, darken, be clouded, be dark, be spotted, (T.M) be thick, (denominative of dammanā)'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the connection with Heb. domen 'dung', Ar. damana 'manure a field' (eig. 'überziehen [Acker]') suggested by Buhl 165 is unlikely. Landberg 1920:848 gives Ar. dmn a basic meaning 'be obscure', but in fact this meaning applies only to G. dammana, while there is no justification for it in the various meanings of Ar. dmn, ib. ETHIOPIC: Tna. dämmāmina, dābäna 'cloud', dāmāna, dābäna, Amh.Arg. dammāna, Gaf. dəmāna, Gur. dāmāna, dābäna, Har. dāna (with m > zero) also in Cushitic: Aw. dammānā, Qem. dāmāna, Had. dubān-čo. Cerulli 1936:245 (followed by Brockelmann 1950:33, and Appleyard, Ab 9[1978].52) connects these nouns with the Cushitic root tum, dum 'be black'. See also Dolgopolsky 1937:51.

meaning: 'obscure, rather cloudy, very gloomy, (T) storehouse' (lit. 'dark place')

origin_explanation: from Gk. adamantos (gen. sg. of adamas 'diamond').

meaning: 'crush, pound, grind, grind to powder, bruise', (T) = Amh. qāṭṭänä 'be fine, be thin' (should be = Amh. aqāṭṭänä 'make thin')
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. damaqa 'hit on the mouth and break the teeth'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. dämmāqä 'crush, squash'.

SEMITIC: Akk. damāqu 'be pleasing', Ug. dmq 'fine, good'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. dämägä 'be pretty', Te. dəmqa 'shine brightly, gleam' (as against Leslau, JAOS 82[1962].3), Amh. dämmāqä 'be bright (color), be lively', Arg. dämmāqa 'be beautiful', Gur. dämäqä, Har. dämäqa 'become lively'.

meaning: 'insert, add, join, associate, unite, mix, mingle, confound, multiply (in arithmetic)'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the root is not to be connected with Syr. dəmārtā 'wonderful, marvelous', as suggested by Brockelmann 1928:158. ETHIOPIC: Te. dämmara 'intermingle, mix, add, sum up, heap up', Tna.Amh. dämmärä; Tna. also däm bärä (with augmented b). The connection with the Amharic root ğmr. ğmr 'begin, start' (for which see Leslau 1979:317) suggested by Praetorius 1879:86 is unlikely. The root passed into Cushitic: Aw. demer gʷa. For the Geez root dmr, see Hammerschmidt 1961:152ff. On an expression in the Anaphora where this root occurs, see J.M. Hanssens, OCP 7 (1941). 206-32.
SEMITIC: possibly Ar. damara 'destroy', Mh. demōr.

meaning: '(T) = Amh. ənba 'bush having a bitter red or yellow fruit''

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. dabbäsä 'be destroyed' (with alter nance of labials). See also damsasa, below.

meaning: (T) 'cover, hide'; damasa (K) 'be dark'; damasa (K) 'dams dark, clouded over, cloudy';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. dammasa 'cover, hide, (XIII form) be cloudy'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. dämmäsä 'tarnish, lose luster', dabäsä (with alternance of labials) 'turn dull or dark', dämäsä 'black cloud'.

meaning: 'abolish, obliterate, erase, de-stroy, quench, blot out, extinguish, wipe out, efface'; tadamasasa, causative; tadamasasa, pass. of damasasa; also 'be lost'; tadamasasa (Lt) 'destroy one another'; damasi, act. part. of damasasa; also 'destroyer'; damasus, pass. part. of damasasa; damäsäse 'destruction, blotting out, oblitera-tion, perdition, annihilation'; madamsas, part. of 'adamasasa;
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the identification with Heb. šmd (hif'il) 'destroy' suggested by Barth 1893:10 is doubtful. ETHIOPIC: Tna. dämäsä 'destroy'. Te. dämäsä. Amh. dämmäsä. See also damasa I.

meaning: variant of dansäwi; see dansa-wa I.

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dozy I, 45 (followed by Noeldeke 1910: 58) considers Ar. dimma-t 'cat' a Geez loanword. In view of the existence of the root in Ar. (Yem) dimm, dimm 'cat', W. Müller considers it a cognate and not a borrowing. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. dəmmu'cat', Amh.Gaf. dəmmat, Tna. dum, utterance used to chase away a cat. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Bil. dummu'cat'. Qem. dämi, Som. dummad.

meaning: (K.Lt) 'issue blood, bleed, become blood' (denominative from dam); 'adammawa 'make bleed';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. dämma 'bleed', Tna. dämwä, Amh. dämma.

SEMITIC: perhaps connected with Syr. daywānā 'evil spirits, demons', from daywā, daywinā 'demoniac'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. demon', Md. daiua, from Persian dēw. ETHIOPIC: Amh. dino, name of an evil spirit. See Mercier 295, n. 18.
from dabäqä 'al, augmented by n.

meaning: (MA) 'wrap up, roll up, make a bundle';

origin_explanation: represents dabbus, above, with augmented n; see also dabbus, above (with nb > mb); cp. Ar. da'u'unbus (Landberg 1920:694).

meaning: (T.Y.M) 'support, sustain'; dänbäz (D.T.M) 'bar, pole, forked pole, prop, support, beam, wooden box for a musical instru-ment, (K*) plectrum for the baganā-harp (Sw. 16) = Amh. sansa 'pole that two men carry on their shoulders in order to transport a heavy object';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. danbaz 'stretcher' (for which see Reinisch 1887:107), Amh. danbuz 'plectrum for the bäganā-harp'.

meaning: (T.M.Sw. in D 1407), dänät (Lt) 'one of the nails of the cross, (T) nail';
origin_explanation: transcription of Latin tenet; see Worrell III 88, n.7; Euringer, ZS 6 (1928).304-5; Strelcyn 1955:80, n.1; and sädor, below.

SEMITIC: Ug. dn 'powerful' (Aistleitner 80), Akk. dannanu 'become strong, become of even thickness', dandanum 'almighty'. ETHIOPIC: Amh. dändäna 'be fat, be stout', Arg. dändädäna. Littmann-Höfner 532 suggest Te. dändäna 'lift heavy things'; pass. Cushitic: Qua. dinädän 'be fat', Kam. diden.

meaning: (K*) 'kind of leaf that serves as a medicine';

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. dardar 'thistle' (G. dandär < dardar by dissimilation; see Brockelmann 1908:226), Heb. dar, Aram. dardar, Syr. dardore, Akk. daddaru 'a thorny plant, wild thistle'. Note that Ar. dardar 'thistle' is considered a transcription of Heb. dardar (Dozy I 432). ETHIOPIC: Tna. dandär 'thistle', Amh. dändär. See also deder.

meaning: 'be terrified, be shocked, be dismayed, be alarmed, be perplexed, be perturbed, be upset, be astonished, be stupefied, be confounded, be amazed, be disturbed in mind, panic, tremble, quake'; 'adangada 'terrify, inspire fear, dismay, strike with terror, alarm, perturb, astonish, amaze'; tadangada 'be struck with consternation'; dängud 'frightened, dismayed, confounded, dis-turbed, alarmed, astonished, terrified, terror-struck'; dängäde 'terror, dismay, astonishment, amaze-ment, dread, stupor, tumult'; dängadännä, same; madangad, part. of 'adangada; also 'terror-spring, terrifying, horrible, stupefying, amaz-ing, astonishing';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the connection between dngd, dngs, dngt and Ar. nagada 'vacillate' suggested by Praetorius 1879:137 is unlikely. Likewise unlikely is the connec-tion with Syr. naqas, eigentlich 'gestossen werden' suggested by Stade 39. ETHIOPIC: Te. dangäsa 'be frightened, be startled, shocked', Tna. dängäsä, Amh. dängägätä, Arg. naggäta, Gur. dənäggätä. Littmann, ZA 33 (1921): 107 (followed by Cerulli 1936:245) compares dngs, dngt with dnbt (for which see Leslau 1979:211); this comparison is correct, the original root would have to be dng't with g' becoming w.b in dnbt. The root passed into Cushitic: Qua. dagat 'be terrified', Khamta dagis.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. dängäl 'papyrus reed which grows in Lake Tana and is used for making boats'.

meaning: (T) = Amh. suf 'sun-flower'; (säge dang`allät 'lily'); ('oda dang`allä 'stem of lily').

(pl. dangägäl) 'chaste (young man), celibate (monk), virgin'; dängäl (pl. dängäläl) 'virgin, maiden'; (dängäl bakale 'twofold virgin', that is, in body and spirit); tadängala 'be a virgin, persevere in virginity' (denominative); dängäläwi 'maidenly'; dängäläwi 'virginity, chastity';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: the derivation of dängäl from ngl (Ar. nagala 'beget') suggested by Praetorius 1879:77 is unlikely. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh.Gur. dängäl 'virgin'; passed into Cushitic: Sa. dingil 'girl who has reached the age of marriage'.

meaning: (K*), dangaza (T.M) 'braid, plait, make curls'; dänguz, däng`äz (pl. dänguzät) 'curl, braid, act of plaiting';
(yədnak) (T.Y.M.Sw. in D 1398) 'cut meat into strips to dry it'; danak (T.K*) 'meat cut into strips';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: from a root dnk 'be short' > 'be cut'; see dənk, below.
from Amh. dənk, same; also in Te.Gur. dənk, Tna. Har. danki, from a root such as Tna. däkädäkä 'be short'. See also danaka, above.
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh. dənk`an 'tent', Te. dəkkan, Arg. dukkän; also in Cushitic: Or. dunkan, Sa. dukän. For Amh. dənk`an Praetorius 1879:99 sug-gests a borrowing from Ar. dukkān 'shop'. As for

meaning: (T.K*.M) 'delimit, mark off a boundary'; tadangaga, passive; dängäg 'edge, border, boundary, rim, brink, bank of a river, seashore, margin'; dängäge, same; dängug 'limited, marked off by boundaries'; tadangago (Lt) 'limitation';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. dängägä 'delineate borders, de-mine, define', Amh. dängägä 'delimit, decree, de-fine (laws)'; also dängägä (with palatalization of g') 'decree, institute a law'. The root also occurs in Cushitic: Qab. Had. dänga. It is from dänga that Gur. däng is borrowed.

meaning: (D.Lt) 'stoop down, bow down, recline, be submissive'; danna 'walk with a bent back'; 'adnana, causative; also 'bow down, bend, be tilted, yield, be submissive'; dənən 'bent, who stoops down, lowly, submissive, subjugated'; dənənne 'inclining, stooping down, subjection'; 'adnani, madnani, part. of 'adnana; madnān 'inclining (n), bending, (KG) place for bending over'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. danna (dnn) 'be crook-backed', Ar. (Dat) danna 'bow down'; related to dny and dn: Ar. danna bira>sihī 'he inclined his head' (for which see Landberg 1920:856), Ar. dana>a 'be low'. Aram. danā 'be lowly, prostrate oneself'. Syr. 'adnā nafšeh 'he submitted himself', Md. dna 'be submissive, prostrate oneself'; also Ug. dn-i 'baseness'. ETHIOPIC: Te. dänna 'incline, stoop', Tna. dänänä, Amh. dännänä 'bend, bend over'.
from Amh. dännäqä, same; also Tna. dännäqä, Gaf. ('ad) dännäqä 'amaze', Har. dänäqä 'be amazing', Gur. ('ad) dännäqä 'surprise'. The root passed into Cushitic: Khamta diniq 'marvelous'.
possibly to be identified with dqq 'be fine, be minute'; see däqäqä. It could also be a misprint for danqawa, for which see danqawa.

from daqat 'event', with nasalization of q through n (Dillmann 932). Tna.Amh.Gur. dänqat 'by chance' < dänqät, with nq > ng (so also Praetorius 1879: 72). On dänqät, see Leslau 1979:213, 314, under ('ad) dännäqä.

meaning: 'be deaf, be hard of hearing, be ignorant, be stupid'; 'adanqawa (K*), causative; dänqäw 'deaf, hard of hearing, simple, plain'; dänqäwe 'deafness'; dänqäwi, dänqäwi 'simple, plain, deaf'; dänqunä (Lt) 'simplicity of character'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Har. dänqa 'deaf', Gaf. dänqu, Q. qā; also in Cushitic: Or. donqo 'stupid', Som. 'fool', Tem. danqa. For the connection between dnqw and dnq"r 'be deaf' in the various Ethiopic languages, see Dillmann 1116, Praetorius 1879:215 (under dänägq"ärä), Gur. For the meanings 'be deaf, be stupid', see Gur. dänägq"ärä (Leslau 1979:215).

either directly from Gk. dēnárion (from Latin denarius) or from Aram.-Syr. dīnārā (see Noeldeke 1910:33, 41; Jeffery 133-5); also Ar. dīnār (for which see Jeffery, ib.). Amh. dinar. On the weight of the dinār, see Pankhurst 1970, 1:55.

meaning: (Y) 'defile, render unclean, pollute'; tadannasa 'be unclean, be defiled, be defile oneself, (T) be a sinner'; dännuş 'dirty, unclean, polluted, impudent, base'; danas 'filth, dirt, defilement, adulteration, unchasteness, corruption'; (za'ənbəla danas 'spotless'); danasä (Lt) 'defilement'
origin_explanation: dänasäwi 'given to unchasteness or foulness'. SEMITIC: Ar. danisa 'be defiled, be polluted'. ETHIOPIC: Te. dännäsä 'defile, render unclean', dännäsä 'damage, disfigure, render inferior'. See also dannasa II.

meaning: 'give rewards, give wages'; tadannasa 'receive rewards'; danas 'reward, hire, wages, price'
origin_explanation: Littmann-Höfner 531 combine dannasa I and dannasa II translating dannasa 'einen Hurenlohn geben', tadannasa 'sich beschmutzen', but in fact dannasa II 'give reward, give hire' is not limited to 'Hurenlohn'.

meaning: (K*), danada 'be envious, be jealous, be stingy'

origin_explanation: see danşawa I

meaning: 'be envious, be jealous, greedy, (Y.T) be stingy, be unfeeling'; tadanaşawa 'envy one another, be jealous one another, contend enviously with one another, (Lt) shout at one another'; danşawi, danşawi 'deceitful, ill-disposed, envious, stingy, avaricious'; danşäw 'envying, holding fast to something'; danşäwe 'stinginess, stint, envy'; madanşäw (Gr) 'envious, jealous'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. dänşäwä 'feel resentment, be displeased'. See also danaşa.

meaning: (T) 'draw the waistband of the trousers tight by pulling the string'; (note that the form of the verb is unusual; it seems to be a denominative from dǝntär; dǝntäro (T), dǝntäro, dǝntär (K*) 'running knot'

origin_explanation: (see Dillmann 1116).
secondary form of dayyana, below; also Amh. dänäyä 'to judge', Tna. dänayä. It is from dny Tna.Amh.Har. dannä is derived.

meaning: 'be numb, be dull, be blunt, be bland, be sluggish, be indolent, languish, be inactive, (T) become deaf'; 'adanzaza (Lt), causative; danzuz 'blunt, dull, numb, bland, sluggish, indolent, inactive'; danzäze 'dullness, bluntness, sluggishness'
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. dänzäza 'grow stiff, be stunned', Tna. dänzäzä 'become numb', Amh. dänzäzä 'be be slow', dännäzä 'be blunt', Arg. dänzäzä.
ETHIOPIC: probably to be identified with Amh. mä-däqadäq 'temporary scaffolding'.
ETHIOPIC: a transferred meaning of Tna. däq"äq"ä 'press, squeeze, wring', Te. däqäqäqa 'split'.

meaning: 'perforate, bore, drill, (Y) twist, (KG) hit'; däqahi (DTW 378), act. participle; madqäh (pl. madäqäh) 'instrument for boring, awl, hammer, drill, vessel used for religious practices' (possibly for maqädäh 'bucket')
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. däqqa 'hit, strike hard'; related to Te. taqha 'rub (tinder, wood)', with d-q > t-q by assimilation, matqah 'fire drill'. The root dqh is also a metathesis in relation to qdh. qadha II. See also daqha II, mdqh I.

meaning: 'bless, consecrate'; madqäh 'consecration, dedication, dedication festival, dedication of a temple'
origin_explanation: Dillmann 1098 connects it with daqha I 'perforate'. For 'consecration, dedication', see also qadha II.

from Ar. daqal < Aramaic (according to Fraenkel 223, but not so Landberg 1920:823), from dqal 'palm tree' (see also Landberg, ib.); also Te. däqäl 'mast', Tna. däqäl, Amh. däqäl, däqäl, passed into Cushitic: Sa.Af. dakal, Be. däqel, Som. dahal. For 'palm tree', cp. Ar. daqal, PBh. deqel, Aram. diqla, Syr. deqlā, Md. diqla.
ETHIOPIC: Te. däqala, Tna. däqala, Amh. diqala, Har. diqala, passed into Cushitic: Bil. dǝq"ala, Or. diqala.

meaning: Tna. dāqāqā 'be refined, be minute', Te. dāqāqā 'be refined, be minute, the ground level, off'. Amh. dāqāqā 'fine gr, be minute'. dāqāqā 'break the ground, level off'). Gur. dāqāqā 'fine gr'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: The root is common with Cushitic. Or. dāka 'grind'. Sid. dak (see Cohen 1947:no 326).
ETHIOPIC: Amh. duq°anduq, duq°adduq 'crowbar', from daqā, with augmented n going back to daq 'crush', below.
from Ar. daqīqa; also in Te. daqīqāt. Amh. dāqīqa.

meaning: 'be sleepy, fall asleep, slumber', 'adaqqaqa, causative'; daqqqus 'one who slumbers, one who is asleep'; daqqās 'sleep, slumber, drowsiness'; mədədqaqās 'place where one sleeps, bed' (note that məntə, the Amh. translation of G. daqqasa given by Tayyä, p.111, is to be corrected into mənnəta);
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: related to Ar. dukās 'sleepiness'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. dāqāqā 'sleep', Amh. dāqāqā Geez).

meaning: (yədaqqaqa) (T.Y.M) 'be small, be a child, be thin'; daqaqa 'crush, pound, grind, powder'; 'adqaqa 'grind, pound, crush, make fine, weak-en'; daqquq 'crushed, pounded, ground fine, thin (voice), weak (voice), small'; daqīq (fem. daqqāq, pl. masc. daqīqān, pl. fem. daqīqāt, daqqāqāt; pl. com. daqīqawəq, daqāwəqt, daqīqāyəq) 'crushed, pounded, ground, slight, thin, minor, small, little, young, child, son, servant, children, little ones (also of animals), offspring, progeny, disciples'; (daqīqa daqīq 'grandchildren'); (daqīqa nabīyāt 'minor prophets'); daqqāq 'newly converted Christians'; daqqāq (fem. of daqīq) 'small particle'; daqq 'little ones, children, sons, servant(s)'; (daqqa ṣərḥ 'chamber servants, eunuchs'); daqqa mazmur 'disciples'); daqīqənnā 'childhood, youth'; madqaq, madqaqt, (T) madqaq 'mortar for grinding. (T) grinding slab'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. daqqāqa 'reduce to powder'. Sar. daqq 'flour'. Soq. Mh. daqq 'pound'. Heb. daq (daqq) 'crush'. Ar. daqq 'crush'. Syr. daqq (daqq) 'reduce to powder, crush'. Md. duq. Ug. dq 'small'. Akk. daqqāqu 'be small', duqququ 'crush', to dkk, dky, dwq. Ar. dakka 'crush'. Soq. dekdek 'beat'. Heb. dākā (dkk). Aram. dekak. Ug. dk 'pound, mix'. Akk. dakāku. Syr. dūqā (dwq) 'something broken'. For 'child', cp. Syr. daq°aqā 'young children', dardaqā (Aram.Syr. by dissimilation). Md. dirdiqia.
misreading of där III given by Tayyä, reprint. See also dor I, II.
from Aram.-Syr. därā. See also dor III.
misprint for dor given in Tayyä.
from Ar. dayr 'monastery', of Syriac (dayrā) origin (see Fraenkel 275); also Amh. där.
from Ar. durr(a) 'pearl'; also in Aram. dūrā, durrā. Md. dura (Fraenkel 58, 210). See also dor II.
perhaps to be connected with dor I 'pearl', the brightness of the pearl referring to the divine fire. See also dar.
comes close to Heb. dōr 'generation', whereas där I is taken from Aram.-Syr. därā.
from Amh. dur 'forest' < dābar (for which see dabara), dabr); also in Tna. dur.

meaning: 'sink down, become nearly flat (roof formerly pitched)'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. därra, same.

meaning: darrə'a 'be determined, be harsh, be severe'.
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Tna. därrə'e 'beat, strike'.

meaning: (pl. 'adrā') 'coat of mail, breastplate, harness'; (darra 'angədā 'breastplate'); darrə'a 'wear a coat of mail' (denominative); tadarrə'a, same; also 'be armed as with a coat of mail'; darrə'u (Lt) 'who wears a coat of mail, armor-plated'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. dir' 'breastplate'. Soq. darah 'put on garment'. Bittner, WZKM 30(1917-18).352, n. 3. Noeldeke 1910:53 thinks that Ar. dir' is borrowed from Geez. ETHIOPIC: Te. där 'coat of mail', Tna. där'i, Amh. dir, där.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. där 'warp'; connected with Tna. där'a 'patch, sew together'. Tna. därrə'e.

meaning: 'throw (a weapon), hurl, hit hard, shoot, pierce (with a weapon)'; tadarbaya (Gr) 'passive'; tadarabāwa 'throw at one another, pierce'; darabāwi (Lt) 'bowman, spearman'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. darbā (dərby) 'throw into (a disagreeable affair)'. Dillmann 1094 compares Heb. dārābān, dorbān 'the (iron) point of a cattle prod'; see on this noun E.Y. Kutscher, Leshonenu 30 (1966). 18. ETHIOPIC: Tna. därbāyā 'throw', Te. därba (dərby), Amh. därābba; also Cushitic: Aw. darbū-g°a 'throw'. Dar. darbe.
Tayyä quoted by M 486 has yərdā.
see darasa.

meaning: (T.Y.MA) = Amh. därrātā, perhaps in the meaning 'be broad-chested' (denominative of därāt 'chest'); darfə'u would then be a denominative of darfə'u 'hump', below. Note that Amh. därrātā also means 'patch, pile one thing atop another (like a patch)'.

origin_explanation: see darfə'a, above.
from Ar. darfil 'dolphin' (for which see Dozy 1 435), from Gk. delphīna, acc. of delphīs 'dolphin'.

meaning: 'be joined together, be united'; darraga (KG) 'join, unite'; 'adraga 'connect, join, assemble, harness'; tadarraga, tadarraga, passive of 'adraga; tadāraga 'be joined to one another'; 'astadāraga 'cause to be joined to one another'.

'connected, joined'; (dərūga 'jointly, together, equally'); darg 'union, connection, conjunction'; (darga, ba-darg 'jointly, together, equally'); dārgat 'conjunction, union, connection, alignment, incorporation, company (business), association, choir'; (dargata 'in a crowd, together'); 'adrāgī (Gr.475) 'one who assembles'; 'adrāgo 'afrās 'the harnessing of horses'; tadārāgī, part. of tadāraga; 'astadārāgī (Gr.475), part. of 'astadāraga.

phonetics: Gr. drakōn 'dragon'; also Amh. darāgon, Aram. syr. darqūnā.
origin_explanation: from Gk. drakōn 'dragon'; also Amh. darāgon, Aram. syr. darqūnā.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. darho. Te. derho, Amh.Arg. doro, Gur. gārā; from Cushitic: Qem.Bil. doroho, Aw. duri, Som. dōro.

from Gk. drakhmē through Persian d(i)rahm and Ar. dirham; also Tna. darhim, dārim, Te. darhām, darhēm. For Aram. darkamōna, Syr. darkamā, see Fraenkel 191, Jeffery 129. Hebrew darkemōn (āt) is an Aramaism. For the value of the Amh. darhām, see Pankhurst 1970, 1:55-6. See also didrakam, darkame.

meaning: 'be rough, be hard, be well-trodden, be stubborn, be savage, be cruel, be harsh, be fierce, become angry'; 'daruk 'hard, harsh, ferocious, fierce savage, cruel, stubborn'; 'madrak 'doorway, doorpost, threshold entrance court, vestibule'; (madrāka hōh 'threshold')
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ug. (m)drk 'rule, dominion' (that is, 'harshness'). In view of G. madrak 'doorway, threshold', it is tempting to connect the root drk with Heb. Aram.-Syr. drk 'tread upon, step upon' (so Dillmann 1095), but the basic meaning of G. daraka is 'be rough, hard' developed into the secondary meaning 'be well-trodden' (hence 'doorway, threshold'), and it is not to be compared with the Semitic root drk 'tread upon'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. dārākā 'press, oppress', Te. dārākā 'push away'; Amh. madrāk 'threshold' is borrowed from Geez.
from Ar. daraga 'rank, stair' in a dialect in which g is pronounced g; also Amh. dārāga 'stairs'.
see also medargu.

meaning: Amh. maṭṭabāqiya 'paste, glue', derived from darga I 'be joined'.
origin_explanation: derived from darga I 'be joined'.

meaning: 'patch, mend'; dargahā, dargahā, (KG) dargah 'small piece of cloth, rag, stuff, new piece of cloth, (KG) patched garment'; dargahā labs 'new cloth'.

origin_explanation: from Gk. darkanos 'madder. Rubia tinctoria, dye made therefrom' (W. Müller). See also didarkano.
transcription of Heb. dārōm.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. darāmo, same. See also şarāmo.

meaning: 'thigh bandages, trousers, drawers, undergarment, loincloth, tunic' (M 496 'without intelligence' is a mistranslation of Tayyā's labbalabba, for in fact it means 'pantaloons' likewise M's rendering 'jealousy' is a mistranslation of Tayyā's qēnat, which also means 'belt'); darmānq(a) zaba'si zahaqqe (Ex. 28:4) 'a man's girth around the hips' (Ludolf 498) stands strangely for Heb. mişnepet, Gk. kidaris 'miter, turban'.
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ludolf 498 and Dillmann 1092 connect with Ar. durmūk, durmūk 'kind of carpet, kind of garment' (for which see Dozy I 438). Fraenkel 289 derives it from Syr. tarbānqā 'tunic' (from Persian tanbānak). He also compares it with Ar. zarmānaqa 'kind of cloak made of wool', and not with durmūk. ETHIOPIC: Amh. darmanq 'trousers that have close-fitting embroidered cuffs'.

meaning: 'kitchen, dining room, storage house, (K) forest' (it translates 'forest' in 3 Kings 7:2 = 1 Kings 7:2);
origin_explanation: from Gk. oikon drýmou 'house of the forest' (oikos, acc. sg. of oikos 'house'; drýmou is the gen. sg. of drýmos 'thicket'. The translation given by T.M.Sw. is strange.
ETHIOPIC: Dillmann 1095 and Praetorius 1879:71 identify it with Amh. darēṭṭ 'partridge'. In case darnaq would go back to darraq, with q > ç (for Cohen 1939:36).
derpa (MA) = Amh. tāmāggābā translated 'eat, feed' but in view of durāp 'chief, leader' it should be rendered 'be administered'.
from Gk. dorkados, gen. sg. of dorkas 'gazelle' (Hommel 1877:XXIX, 86). See also darqās.

phonetics: (D), darqiqon (K), duriqon (L.D.T), darqiqon, darqeqon, darqiqon (L.D.T), darqiqon, darqeqon, darqiqon (K.D.T) (KG)
meaning: 'one of the seven heavens, (T.K) the seventh heaven, (KG) the sixth heaven'
origin_explanation: see also darteqon.

phonetics: (T.M), dorqās (K.Sw. in D 1407), dorākās (Sw. in D 1407)
meaning: 'antelope, hide of antelope worn as garment, shawl-like garment made of a single layer of cloth';
origin_explanation: from Gk. dorkas 'gazelle'. See also darqodas.

meaning: 'eat dinner', tadarra, tadarrara 'eat dinner, dine'; dorār 'dinner, supper, supper time, food, vigil'; (dorāra som 'eve of Lent, fast on the eve of a holiday')
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. darār 'meal', Te. (ta)darrāra 'find nourishment, eat', Tna. (tā)darrāra 'eat dinner', Amh. dorār 'eve of a festival, supper eaten at that time'. Praetorius, ZDMG 47 (1893), 387 derives it, with reservations, from Cushitic: Or. darāra 'midday meal'. The root is possibly common with Cushitic: Bil. derār 'dinner', Af. dirār.
ETHIOPIC: Te. dāriro 'kind of grain', Amh. dorar 'a variety of sorghum'; also in Cushitic: Sa. dāriro.

meaning: 'compose a religious treatise, compose a book, discuss, interpret'; 'adrāsa (K*), causative'

origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. darasa 'study' (considered by Jeffery 128-9 to be of Hebrew origin; see also Landberg 1920:750ff), Soq. deros 'study', Heb. dāraš 'seek, study', Aram.Syr. darāš 'examine, interpret', Md. drāš 'teach, instruct', Ug. drš 'seek'. For Syr. madrašā 'school' being borrowed from Arabic, see Schwallg, ZDMG 52 (1898).137-8. ETHIOPIC: G. darasa is considered by Noeldeke 1910:38 a Syriac loanword. Ullendorff 1968:97, n. 5 (following Polotsky) thinks that the Ethiopic forms 'tally with their Hebrew rather than their Syriac counterparts'; cp. also Te. dārsa 'recite, read', Tna. dārāsā, Amh. dārrāsā 'compose a book'. For its connection with Gur. dārāsā 'praise in song, sing and dance', see Leslau 1979:221. The root passed into Cushitic: Bil. dris 'preach'.

ETHIOPIC: Te. (mä)drās 'resting place (of cows, of goats)', Te. dārsa 'lie down, rest (cows)', perhaps Amh. baldārās 'stable master'.
from Gk. drỹos, gen.sg. of drỹs 'oak tree'. See also daryos.
from Ar. dār ṣīnī of Persian (dār čīnī) origin; also Amh. dārsīnī, dārsāne 'an odoriferous and medical tree, cinnamon'.
ETHIOPIC: Amh. dārrājā 'ravage, destroy (by raiding)'.
see also māridartiqon, dāriqon. For the ending -qon, see lewegen (ending -qen), one of the names of the fifth heaven.
from Gk. drỹi, dat. sg. of drỹs 'oak tree'. See also daryos.
from dǝbrayāmin, above.
from Gk. drỹos, gen.sg. of drỹs 'oak tree'. See also dars, darāy.
from Gk. diorizon (teikhos) 'separating (wall)'.
ETHIOPIC: Tna.Amh.Gur. das, Har. dās, from Cushitic: Sa.Som.Af. dās, Bil. dōsā. See also dāḥs.
from Gk. despota, voc.sg. of despotēs 'master, lord'.

meaning: 'evil spirit or demon that brings about a disease, (T) pleurisy';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Amh. dāsk, dāsq. See Worrell III.138, Eurzinger, ZS 6 (1928).97, Strelcyn 1955:44 Mercier 282ff.

meaning: 'stag, kind of antelope, (Sw. in D 1098) buffalo';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. dāskānā 'buffalo'. See also Hommel 390, 392.
from Gk. dasypoda, acc. sg. of dasypous 'hare'; also in Aram. dāšipī (Krauss 186).
from Gk. diskon, acc. of diskos 'dish that serves as a portable table'; also Chr.Ar. disqa, disqisa, disqos, Aram. disqōs, disqā.
SEMITIC: Amh. dāssäsā, (tän)dassäsā, dasäsā 'be bent over, lean to one side, sink down'.
SEMITIC: Ar. dassa 'conceal'.

SEMITIC: Ar. dassa 'touch, feel with the hand'. ETHIOPIC: Te. dāssäsā 'touch', Amh. dāssäsā, sā 'touch, feel, grope one's way'; related to dāhsäsā 'touch'. The root passed into Cushitic: dāsäs, dāse 'touch, feel', Bil. dahas.
ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna. dāset 'island', Amh. dāssət, dāssīt; passed into Cushitic: Bil.Sa. dāsēt.
SEMITIC: related to Ar. dāsa (dws) 'tread on', Aram.Syr. dāš (dwš) 'trample, thrash', Md. Akk. dāšu; is also related to Sem. dhs, for which dahasa, above. ETHIOPIC: related to G. (ta)dahasa, above.

meaning: 'region, territory, district, area, boundary, border, frontier, lim it';
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: Te. dawāl 'district, country'. It is tempt ing to connect it with Amh. dāwāl 'periphery of the church parish', but Amh. dāwāl is probably an extension of meaning of dāwāl 'bell', specifically the church bell' > 'area around the church bell'.

SEMITIC: Brockelmann 1928:144 connects it with Syr. dāl (dwl) 'move, quiver'. ETHIOPIC: Te.Tna.Amh.Gur. dāwāl 'bell', Har. dāwāl; 'ring a bell': Te. dāwālā, dāwānā, Tna. Gur. dāwālā; also in Cushitic: Bil. dāwalā 'bell', Sa.Som. dawan, Sid. dūwāla.

meaning: 'be a boundary, be a border contiguous, be limitrophe';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Dillmann 1123 connects it with Ar. dāla (dwl) 'turn'. The connection with Md. dul 'move about', Akk. dālu 'wander about aimlessly', sug gested by Dietrich 295, is doubtful.

meaning: 'stir a fire, place wood on a fire, burn';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. (Dat) dāra (dwr), dahar 'stir the fire' (for which see Landberg 1920:859).
from the name of David; also Syr. dāwīt, Ethiopic dawit.

meaning: 'be sick, be ill, be diseased, fall sick, suffer, be sorrowful';
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. dawiya 'be diseased', Heb. dāwā (dwy), Aram. dāwā, dāwē, Syr. dāwē (dwy), Md. dwa 'be wretched, be miserable'. Ug. dw 'sick', dwy 'be sick'; von Soden 166 compares Akk. dawū, damū 'stagger'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. dāwāyā 'become sick', Amh. dāwwāyā (from Geez); Te. dāwa 'give a medicine, cure' is taken from Ar. dāwā. The root passed into Cushitic: Qem. duwā 'disease'.

SEMITIC: Ar. dawiya 'be diseased', Heb. dāwā (dwy), Aram. dāwā, dāwē, Syr. dāwē (dwy), Md. dwa 'be wretched, be miserable'. Ug. dw 'sick', dwy 'be sick'; von Soden 166 compares Akk. dawū, damū 'stagger'. ETHIOPIC: Tna. dāwāyā 'become sick', Amh. dāwwāyā (from Geez); Te. dāwa 'give a medicine, cure' is taken from Ar. dāwā. The root passed into Cushitic: Qem. duwā 'disease'.

meaning: 'Zeus', (T) name of a star; dǝyās, dǝyos (D 1407,T), same
origin_explanation: From Gk. Zeus 'Zeus', gen. dios, acc. dia.
ETHIOPIC: Tna. dayyābā 'ascend, climb', Te. (ta)dāy-yābā 'swell up (body)'; perhaps from diba 'on, over', above.
from Gk. diabolos 'devil'; also in Te.Tna.Amh. diyablos, Gur. dabilos.

meaning: 'area, region, out-skirts, environs, borders, limits, bordering cities, confines, district, province'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: perhaps Ar. dawwama 'move in a circle, turn'. See also madyām.

meaning: 'judge, sentence, punish, con-demn, convict, damn'; tadayyana, passive; dayyāni 'judge'; dayyūn (K*) 'judged, convicted'; dayn 'judgment, damnation, condemnation, doom'
origin_explanation: SEMITIC: Ar. dāna (dyn) 'pass judgment, condemn', SAr.(Qat) dyn 'judgment, punishment', Heb. dān (dyn) 'judge' (v), Md. dūn 'judge' (v), daiana 'judge' (n), Ug. dyn 'judge' (v), Akk. diānu, dānu 'judge' (v), dayyānu 'judge' (n). Noeldeke 1910:39 thinks that G. dayn 'judgment', Ar.Heb. dīn, Ar.Heb. dayyān 'judge' (n) are taken from Aramaic. Zimmern 23-4 is of the opinion that Akk. dēnu, dīnu 'judgment', dānu 'judge' is the origin of the Semitic root. Kaufman 43, n. 58 thinks that the root dyn is common Semitic. For Ar. dīn 'religion, judgment', see Jeffery 132. ETHIOPIC: Tna. dāyyānā 'condemn', danāyā 'pass judgment', dayna, dañña 'judge'. Te. danya, dayna 'mediator', (ta)dāna 'accept a verdict', Amh. daññā 'arbitrate, judge', Amh.Arg.Har. dañña 'judge' (n), Gur. daññā. See also dānaya.

meaning: 'lie in ambush'; dipā 'ambush, lying in wait, snare'
origin_explanation: see dabaya.
transcription of Heb. dāyeq.
(M's dayāq is a misreading of Tayyā's doyaq); from Amh. dayyāq 'large waterskin of tanned hide'

meaning: 'deacon' (see Walker 105-9, Hammerschmidt 1967:120, 130); (liqa diyāqon, liqa diyāqonāt 'archdeacon'); (rǝ'sa diyāqonāt 'archdeacon'); (nǝfqa diyāqon, manfaqa diyāqon 'sub-dea-con'); diyāqonāy 'deacon'; diyāqonāwit, diyāqonāyit 'deaconess'; diyāqonnā, diyāqonnāt 'diaconate'; diqqunnā, diqqʷǝnnā 'diaconate'; dāyāqʷana (D), deqona (K*) 'become deacon' (denominative)
origin_explanation: from Gk. diakōn, diakonos 'deacon'; also in Te.Tna. Amh.Gur. diyāqon, Chr.-Ar. diyākun, Syr. diyāqun. See also zeqʷana, zǝyāqʷana, with a variant z:d.
from Gk. diaspora 'dispersion'.

meaning: 'string or belt of colored glass beads worn about the waist'

origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: perhaps Amh. dāzāddāzā 'thrash'.

meaning: 'bell'; (note that the print is not clear; it could be dizyāhu or dezyāhu)
origin_explanation: ETHIOPIC: related to Amh. dāzāddāzā 'sound the drum', dǝzdǝz 'sistrum'. See also muzyāhu.
